title: Inheritance and Testimony
subtitle: How Islamic and Biblical law address the distribution of estates and the requirements for legal witnesses
summary: Islamic law and Biblical law both establish detailed rules for inheritance and the role of testimony in legal matters, though they arrive at different distributions based on their respective theological and social frameworks. This article explores the scriptural bases, scholarly interpretations, and underlying principles that guide these systems in both traditions.

seo.metaTitle: Inheritance and Testimony in Islam and the Bible
seo.metaDescription: Comparative analysis of Islamic and Biblical inheritance law and testimony requirements, with scriptural basis and scholarly interpretation.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.body: Both Islamic and Christian legal traditions recognize that the transfer of property after death and the role of witnesses in establishing truth are matters of fundamental social importance. These systems emerged from specific scriptural texts and were developed through centuries of scholarly interpretation. While both traditions emphasize justice and fairness, they have arrived at different rules about how estates should be divided and what weight different testimonies should carry. Understanding these differences requires engaging with the scriptural foundations, the historical contexts in which laws developed, and the reasoning offered by scholars within each tradition. Rather than viewing these as competing systems of right and wrong, we can appreciate how each tradition approaches the shared challenge of ensuring just outcomes in matters affecting families and communities.

sections.title: Islamic Inheritance Law
sections.body:
Islamic inheritance law, known as Faraaiz (plural of Farida, meaning obligation or duty), is one of the most precisely detailed areas of Islamic jurisprudence. It rests on the principle that the distribution of an estate is not a matter of personal choice but a divine ordinance. The Quran provides the foundational rules in Surah An-Nisaa (The Women), with the most comprehensive statement appearing in verses 4:11-12. These verses establish fixed proportions based on familial relationship and gender.

The central principle is that male heirs receive twice the share of female heirs in most cases, though this operates within a complex system where different relatives have different priorities. A son receives twice what a daughter receives, but only if both are in the same category of heirs. If there are only daughters and no sons, daughters collectively inherit two-thirds of the estate. A single daughter inherits half. Parents receive specific shares depending on whether children survive.

Some critics contend that a 1:2 female-to-male inheritance ratio appears to embed a judgment about women's inherently reduced entitlements to property, independent of other circumstances. Islamic scholars, however, identify several theological principles underlying this distribution: (1) Males typically carry the financial responsibility for maintaining female relatives, so the additional share accounts for this obligation; (2) The system prevents excessive fragmentation of family property; (3) It recognizes the economic interdependence of family members; (4) The precise rules prevent disputes and ensure predictability.

The inheritance rules apply only after bequests (up to one-third of the estate) and debts are settled. Spouses, parents, and children are considered primary heirs, while siblings and other relatives inherit only in specific circumstances when no primary heirs remain. The calculation becomes complex when multiple categories of heirs exist simultaneously, requiring sophisticated mathematical proportioning that Muslim scholars developed into a detailed science.

sections.title: Islamic Law on Testimony and Witnesses
sections.body:
Testimony (shahada) holds a central place in Islamic law and ethics. The Quran emphasizes the gravity of bearing false witness and the importance of honest testimony in establishing the truth. The most extensive Quranic treatment appears in Surah Al-Baqara verse 2:282, the longest single verse in the Quran, which addresses the documentation of financial transactions.

This verse prescribes detailed procedures for recording debts and commercial agreements. Significantly, it states that testimony should come from two men, or one man and two women—the latter configuration described as sufficient if one woman forgets, the other can remind her. Some critics contend that this specification appears to embed a judgment about women's reduced legal reliability or capacity for commercial judgment, independent of any contextual explanation. Scholars have debated the reasoning behind this proportioning extensively. Some argue it reflects practical circumstances in the 7th century Arab context; others contend it reflects fundamental principles about judgment and precision. Many contemporary Islamic scholars note that the verse's primary concern is ensuring accuracy and reliability, achieved through documentation and multiple witnesses.

Islamic jurisprudence developed detailed rules about who can serve as a witness (requirements for moral character, mental capacity, freedom, and Muslim faith in certain cases), what types of testimony are admissible in different contexts, and how testimony should be weighed. In inheritance matters specifically, witnesses to a will or testament must meet high standards of character and probity. Different schools of Islamic law (madhabs) have developed varying applications of these principles, though the core commitment to reliability and truthfulness remains constant.

It is crucial to note that the testimony rule in verse 2:282 appears specifically in the context of commercial documentation and transactions. Scholars distinguish between this context and other legal matters. Additionally, many Muslim-majority countries and contemporary Islamic scholars argue that applying this ratio rigidly in all modern legal contexts contradicts the underlying principle of achieving reliability and justice, particularly when women have achieved educational parity and equal access to knowledge.

sections.title: Biblical Inheritance Law
sections.body:
Biblical inheritance law is more varied and contextual than Islamic law because the Hebrew Bible does not provide a single comprehensive legal code for inheritance. Instead, legal principles emerge across multiple books of the Torah, illustrated through specific cases and general principles. The cases of the daughters of Zelophehad recorded in Numbers 27 and Numbers 36 are particularly instructive.

These passages address the situation of daughters who have no brothers and must inherit their father's property. The Israelite legal tradition typically followed primogeniture—the firstborn son inherited a double portion, while other sons shared the remainder. If no sons existed, daughters could inherit, but they faced the constraint that marriage could not remove property from their tribe. Numbers 36 specifies that daughters inheriting property must marry within their paternal tribe to preserve tribal boundaries.

This case law reveals principles underlying biblical inheritance: (1) Property remained within family lines; (2) Males held primary responsibility for family welfare; (3) Inheritance served to maintain tribal and economic stability; (4) The system could adapt to circumstances where male heirs did not exist. Unlike Islamic law's universal mathematical formula, biblical law worked through case-by-case application of these principles.

Critics raise a comparably serious objection here: daughters inherit only as a fallback when no sons exist, rather than possessing an independent right to a portion of the estate regardless of whether brothers are living. Furthermore, Numbers 36's requirement that an inheriting daughter marry within her father's tribe imposes a legal constraint on her marital choice specifically because she holds property as a woman—a restriction male heirs never face. On this reading, the biblical system does not merely happen to favor sons; it treats a daughter's property and her marriage as matters requiring special tribal oversight in a way a son's are not. Mainstream Jewish and Christian interpreters typically respond that Numbers 36 addresses a structural concern about tribal land tenure in a specific agrarian, tribally organized society—preventing land from passing permanently out of a tribe's allotment—rather than expressing a judgment about women's capacity or worth, and they note that the Zelophehad narrative is framed as an expansion of daughters' inheritance rights beyond what the law had previously specified, not a limitation of them. Both readings remain live in scholarly and religious discussion.

The New Testament provides little specific guidance on inheritance, reflecting the early Christian community's expectation of Christ's imminent return and its emphasis on spiritual wealth over material possessions. Luke 12:13-14 records Jesus declining to arbitrate an inheritance dispute, redirecting focus to spiritual matters. However, Paul addresses family support obligations, suggesting that providing for parents and relatives remains a moral duty.

Biblical law generally favored male heirs, but not as an absolute rule—circumstances and adaptability are evident throughout the scriptural examples. Women could own property (Proverbs 31's description of an industrious woman), control resources (Lydia in Acts), and in some contexts inherit. The system was less universally codified than Islamic inheritance law.

sections.title: Biblical Law on Testimony and Witnesses
sections.body:
The Hebrew Bible establishes that testimony must be established by multiple independent witnesses rather than a single person. Deuteronomy 19:15 is the foundational statement: "One witness shall not rise up against a man for any iniquity, or for any sin...At the mouth of two witnesses, or at the mouth of three witnesses, shall a matter be established." This principle appears consistently throughout biblical legal material.

Unlike Islamic law's specification of gender ratios in testimony, biblical law does not explicitly differentiate testimony by gender in its foundational legal texts. The focus is on multiplicity and reliability. Two or three independent witnesses corroborate a matter; one witness alone is insufficient to establish guilt or obligation. The concern is ensuring that accusations and claims are verified, not that they come from specific categories of people.

It is worth noting, however, that the later Jewish legal tradition built on this same textual corpus did not treat the foundational texts' silence on gender as establishing equal testimonial standing in practice. The first-century Jewish historian Josephus states plainly that testimony from women should not be accepted, attributing this to what he calls "the levity and boldness of their sex" (Antiquities of the Jews 4.8.15), and later Mishnaic and Talmudic law (e.g., Mishnah Shevuot 4:1) broadly excluded women as witnesses in most legal proceedings—arguably a more categorical exclusion than the Quran's admit-with-corroboration approach. Rabbinic practice on this point was not perfectly uniform across every time and place, and Christian legal practice diverged further as it moved away from Torah-based procedure, but the historical record shows that the biblical text's silence on gender in testimony did not translate into settled gender-neutral practice within the tradition that produced these texts.

The Gospel of Matthew records Jesus affirming this principle, stating that disputes should be brought before witnesses: "At the mouth of two or three witnesses every word may be established." This continuity from Torah through the New Testament indicates the principle's fundamental importance to the tradition.

In practice, the quality of witnesses mattered significantly. A person known for dishonesty was not acceptable as a witness. Witnesses were often questioned to test their knowledge and consistency. The burden of reliability fell on the witness individually rather than on a systematic calculation of how many witnesses of which gender were required. The biblical emphasis is on achieving truth through multiple independent corroborating accounts rather than through mathematical ratios based on demographic categories.

One important contextual note: biblical law developed in societies where formal education was limited and disproportionately gendered. Legal procedures reflected these realities. As education and social participation changed over time, the reasoning behind witness requirements shifted. Modern Christian ethical reflection generally emphasizes the principle underlying the rule—that truth is established through reliable testimony—rather than requiring rigid adherence to prescriptions shaped by ancient social conditions.

sections.title: Comparative Analysis: Underlying Principles
sections.body:
Both Islamic and Biblical traditions share core commitments while diverging in implementation. Both prioritize justice and fairness. Both recognize that property must be distributed in ways that maintain family cohesion and prevent excessive fragmentation. Both demand reliability in testimony. Yet they embody these commitments differently.

Islamic law chose universalization: a mathematical formula applicable across all circumstances, derived from Quranic verses understood as establishing timeless principles. This approach provides predictability, reduces disputes, and ensures that personal preference cannot override divine ordinance. The gender differential in testimony and inheritance reflects theological commitments about gender roles and responsibilities understood as divinely ordained.

Biblical law chose contextualization: principles applied case-by-case through judges and elders, adapted to circumstances, illustrated through specific situations. This approach provides flexibility and allows circumstances to be weighed, but it also requires ongoing judgment and interpretation. The biblical system does not establish universal mathematical formulas but rather guides decision-makers through principles.

Regarding the treatment of women in inheritance, both traditions historically privileged males, but for somewhat different reasons. Islamic law explicitly grounds this in a principle about financial responsibility: those who must provide for others receive a larger share. Biblical law assumes male primacy without explicit textual justification for the principle. This structural difference creates distinct interpretive pathways: Islamic scholars can debate whether the underlying principle of financial responsibility remains applicable when economic roles have changed. Biblical law, structured through case-based judicial reasoning, similarly permits reexamination of outcomes in light of changed circumstances, though without reference to an articulated underlying principle. Both traditions thus possess mechanisms for responding to social change, each appropriate to its own jurisprudential framework.

On testimony, Islamic law's gender differentiation in the specific context of financial documentation appears linked to historical assumptions about education and experience in commerce. Biblical law's gender-neutral framework, at least as expressed in the foundational legal texts, reflects a different approach: rather than calibrating witness-weight by demographics, establish truth through multiplicity and cross-examination—though, as discussed above, the later rabbinic interpretive tradition built on these same texts did not always preserve that textual neutrality in actual practice. Both approaches aim at the same goal—reliability—through different mechanisms.

Contemporary scholarship in both traditions grapples with these differences. Some Muslim scholars argue that the underlying principle of reliability in testimony can be achieved through different means in contexts where education and professional participation are gender-equal. Some Christian scholars argue that biblical principles should adapt to changed circumstances while maintaining core commitments to family welfare and honest testimony. These are good-faith interpretive debates within each tradition, not failures of either system.

sections.title: Modern Legal Implementations and Scholarly Debate
sections.body:
Contemporary Muslim-majority legal systems vary significantly in how they implement Islamic inheritance law. Some nations have preserved classical Islamic law with minimal modification. Others have reformed it substantially. Notable examples include Morocco and Tunisia, which have revised inheritance law to provide more equal treatment of male and female heirs, justifying these changes through reinterpretation of Islamic principles. These countries argue that Islamic law's core commitment to justice can be served through different distributions when social and economic circumstances have changed.

Modern Islamic scholars debate these reforms vigorously. Some argue that changing the fixed shares violates the explicit language of Quranic verses and Islamic tradition. Others contend that the Quran's principles (justice, fairness, family welfare) can be achieved through different mechanisms, and that maintenance obligations are no longer exclusively male. The debate is sophisticated and principled, not simply progressive versus conservative.

Regarding testimony, most modern Muslim-majority legal systems have adopted gender-neutral evidence standards for many contexts, while some preserve gender-differentiated testimony specifically for certain religious matters. Islamic scholars justify these different approaches by distinguishing contexts: the Quranic rule about testimony may apply specifically to religious or commercial matters where it was revealed, while criminal and civil litigation may operate under different principles.

Biblical legal traditions in Christian societies have long since moved away from biblical inheritance law as such. Modern secular legal systems governing Christian-majority nations use entirely different inheritance frameworks based on testamentary freedom and legal formalism. Christian scholars discuss what biblical principles suggest about just inheritance but do not typically advocate restoring biblical law as written. The tradition has been absorbed into broader Western legal evolution.

On testimony, the biblical principle of requiring corroboration survives in modern law through the "beyond a reasonable doubt" standard and the right to cross-examination, though the specific witness requirements have been superseded by professional forensics, documentary evidence, and other verification methods. The underlying principle—that serious claims require solid evidence—endures even as the mechanisms have changed dramatically.

Both traditions face the hermeneutical challenge of distinguishing between timeless principles and historically situated applications. This is not evasion but careful interpretation: determining what aspects of a law reflect unchanging divine justice and what aspects reflect contingent solutions to particular contexts. Muslims and Christians across the spectrum of traditionalism to reformism engage this challenge seriously.

sections.title: Scriptural Sources and Scholarly Interpretation
sections.body:
The Islamic sources for inheritance law rest primarily on Quran 4:11-12, supplemented by hadith (reports of the Prophet Muhammad's practice and teaching) and centuries of jurisprudential development. Early Islamic jurisprudence established the science of Faraaiz, with scholars like Muhammad ibn Jarir al-Tabari (d. 310/923) and later jurists creating systematic treatments. The four main schools of Islamic law (Hanafi, Maliki, Shafi'i, Hanbali) developed slightly different applications while maintaining the core proportions established in the Quranic text.

On testimony, Quran 2:282 provides the most detailed source, though verses 65:2 and other passages also address witness requirements. The hadith collections contain numerous traditions about honest testimony and the prohibition of false witness. Islamic jurisprudence developed detailed evidentiary rules considering context, the character of witnesses, and the nature of the matter under judgment.

Biblical sources for inheritance law are dispersed throughout the Torah and Prophets. Numbers 27 and 36 provide the case law about daughters inheriting. Deuteronomy 21:17 establishes the double portion for the firstborn. Genesis contains narratives about inheritance (Esau and Jacob's birthright, Joseph's double portion as Jacob's favored son) that illustrate but do not systematically legislate. The Proverbs contain wisdom about property and family. The New Testament offers limited specific guidance on inheritance mechanics.

On testimony, Deuteronomy 19:15-19 provides the foundational requirement for multiple witnesses and establishes consequences for false testimony. The law treats bearing false witness as a serious crime. Proverbs repeatedly emphasizes honest testimony as part of righteous conduct. The New Testament affirms the principle of corroborating witnesses without adding systematic procedural requirements.

Mediaeval Islamic jurisprudence saw sophisticated development of these principles. Al-Sarakhsi (d. 490/1096) and other Hanafi jurists wrote detailed works on evidence and testimony. The Maliki tradition developed equally nuanced approaches. These scholarly traditions continue today with contemporary Muslim jurists addressing modern contexts like digital evidence, anonymous reporting, and equal education.

Mediaeval Christian scholarship on inheritance and testimony developed differently because Christian societies were not primarily governed by biblical law after Constantine. Theological reflection on biblical principles continued, but implementation occurred through canon law (the law of the Church) and civil law (increasingly secular). Thomas Aquinas and other medieval theologians discussed biblical principles, but their concern was moral theology rather than legal implementation. This divergence between Islamic legal tradition (which remained primary in Muslim societies) and Christian theological tradition (which became secondary to civil law) represents a major historical difference between the traditions.

quranVerses.arabic: يُوصِيكُمُ ٱللَّهُ فِىٓ أَوْلَٰدِكُمْ ۖ لِلذَّكَرِ مِثْلُ حَظِّ ٱلْأُنثَيَيْنِ ۚ فَإِن كُنَّ نِسَآءًۭ فَوْقَ ٱثْنَتَيْنِ فَلَهُنَّ ثُلُثَا مَا تَرَكَ ۖ وَإِن كَانَتْ وَٰحِدَةًۭ فَلَهَا ٱلنِّصْفُ ۚ وَلِأَبَوَيْهِ لِكُلِّ وَٰحِدٍۢ مِّنْهُمَا ٱلسُّدُسُ مِمَّا تَرَكَ إِن كَانَ لَهُۥ وَلَدٌۭ ۚ فَإِن لَّمْ يَكُن لَّهُۥ وَلَدٌۭ وَوَرِثَهُۥٓ أَبَوَاهُ فَلِأُمِّهِ ٱلثُّلُثُ ۚ فَإِن كَانَ لَهُۥٓ إِخْوَةٌۭ فَلِأُمِّهِ ٱلسُّدُسُ ۚ مِنۢ بَعْدِ وَصِيَّةٍۢ يُوصِى بِهَآ أَوْ دَيْنٍ ۗ ءَابَآؤُكُمْ وَأَبْنَآؤُكُمْ لَا تَدْرُونَ أَيُّهُمْ أَقْرَبُ لَكُمْ نَفْعًۭا ۚ فَرِيضَةًۭ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ عَلِيمًا حَكِيمًۭا
quranVerses.translation: Allah instructs you concerning your children: for the male, what is equal to the share of two females. But if there are [only] daughters, two or more, for them is two thirds of one's estate. And if there is only one, for her is half. And for one's parents, to each one of them is a sixth of his estate if he left children. But if he had no children and the parents [alone] inherit from him, then for his mother is one third. And if he had brothers [or sisters], for his mother is a sixth, after any bequest he [may have] made or debt. Your parents or your children - you know not which of them are nearest to you in benefit. [These shares are] an obligation [imposed] by Allah. Indeed, Allah is ever Knowing and Wise.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: ۞ وَلَكُمْ نِصْفُ مَا تَرَكَ أَزْوَٰجُكُمْ إِن لَّمْ يَكُن لَّهُنَّ وَلَدٌۭ ۚ فَإِن كَانَ لَهُنَّ وَلَدٌۭ فَلَكُمُ ٱلرُّبُعُ مِمَّا تَرَكْنَ ۚ مِنۢ بَعْدِ وَصِيَّةٍۢ يُوصِينَ بِهَآ أَوْ دَيْنٍۢ ۚ وَلَهُنَّ ٱلرُّبُعُ مِمَّا تَرَكْتُمْ إِن لَّمْ يَكُن لَّكُمْ وَلَدٌۭ ۚ فَإِن كَانَ لَكُمْ وَلَدٌۭ فَلَهُنَّ ٱلثُّمُنُ مِمَّا تَرَكْتُم ۚ مِّنۢ بَعْدِ وَصِيَّةٍۢ تُوصُونَ بِهَآ أَوْ دَيْنٍۢ ۗ وَإِن كَانَ رَجُلٌۭ يُورَثُ كَلَٰلَةً أَوِ ٱمْرَأَةٌۭ وَلَهُۥٓ أَخٌ أَوْ أُخْتٌۭ فَلِكُلِّ وَٰحِدٍۢ مِّنْهُمَا ٱلسُّدُسُ ۚ فَإِن كَانُوٓا۟ أَكْثَرَ مِن ذَٰلِكَ فَهُمْ شُرَكَآءُ فِى ٱلثُّلُثِ ۚ مِنۢ بَعْدِ وَصِيَّةٍۢ يُوصَىٰ بِهَآ أَوْ دَيْنٍ غَيْرَ مُضَآرٍّۢ ۚ وَصِيَّةًۭ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَلِيمٌۭ
quranVerses.translation: And for you is half of what your wives leave if they have no child. But if they have a child, for you is one fourth of what they leave, after any bequest they [may have] made or debt. And for the wives is one fourth if you leave no child. But if you leave a child, then for them is an eighth of what you leave, after any bequest you [may have] made or debt. And if a man or woman leaves neither ascendants nor descendants but has a brother or a sister, then for each one of them is a sixth. But if they are more than two, they share a third, after any bequest which was made or debt, as long as there is no detriment [caused]. [This is] an ordinance from Allah, and Allah is Knowing and Forbearing.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: يَٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِذَا تَدَايَنتُم بِدَيْنٍ إِلَىٰٓ أَجَلٍۢ مُّسَمًّۭى فَٱكْتُبُوهُ ۚ وَلْيَكْتُب بَّيْنَكُمْ كَاتِبٌۢ بِٱلْعَدْلِ ۚ وَلَا يَأْبَ كَاتِبٌ أَن يَكْتُبَ كَمَا عَلَّمَهُ ٱللَّهُ ۚ فَلْيَكْتُبْ وَلْيُمْلِلِ ٱلَّذِى عَلَيْهِ ٱلْحَقُّ وَلْيَتَّقِ ٱللَّهَ رَبَّهُۥ وَلَا يَبْخَسْ مِنْهُ شَيْـًۭٔا ۚ فَإِن كَانَ ٱلَّذِى عَلَيْهِ ٱلْحَقُّ سَفِيهًا أَوْ ضَعِيفًا أَوْ لَا يَسْتَطِيعُ أَن يُمِلَّ هُوَ فَلْيُمْلِلْ وَلِيُّهُۥ بِٱلْعَدْلِ ۚ وَٱسْتَشْهِدُوا۟ شَهِيدَيْنِ مِن رِّجَالِكُمْ ۖ فَإِن لَّمْ يَكُونَا رَجُلَيْنِ فَرَجُلٌۭ وَٱمْرَأَتَانِ مِمَّن تَرْضَوْنَ مِنَ ٱلشُّهَدَآءِ أَن تَضِلَّ إِحْدَىٰهُمَا فَتُذَكِّرَ إِحْدَىٰهُمَا ٱلْأُخْرَىٰ ۚ وَلَا يَأْبَ ٱلشُّهَدَآءُ إِذَا مَا دُعُوا۟ ۚ وَلَا تَسْـَٔمُوٓا۟ أَن تَكْتُبُوهُ صَغِيرًا أَوْ كَبِيرًا إِلَىٰٓ أَجَلِهِۦ ۚ ذَٰلِكُمْ أَقْسَطُ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ وَأَقْوَمُ لِلشَّهَٰدَةِ وَأَدْنَىٰٓ أَلَّا تَرْتَابُوٓا۟ ۖ إِلَّآ أَن تَكُونَ تِجَٰرَةً حَاضِرَةًۭ تُدِيرُونَهَا بَيْنَكُمْ فَلَيْسَ عَلَيْكُمْ جُنَاحٌ أَلَّا تَكْتُبُوهَا ۗ وَأَشْهِدُوٓا۟ إِذَا تَبَايَعْتُمْ ۚ وَلَا يُضَآرَّ كَاتِبٌۭ وَلَا شَهِيدٌۭ ۚ وَإِن تَفْعَلُوا۟ فَإِنَّهُۥ فُسُوقٌۢ بِكُمْ ۗ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ۖ وَيُعَلِّمُكُمُ ٱللَّهُ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمٌۭ
quranVerses.translation: O you who have believed, when you contract a debt for a specified term, write it down. And let a scribe write [it] between you in justice. Let no scribe refuse to write as Allah has taught him. So let him write and let the one who has the obligation dictate. And let him fear Allah, his Lord, and not leave anything out of it. But if the one who has the obligation is of limited understanding or weak or unable to dictate himself, then let his guardian dictate in justice. And bring to witness two witnesses from among your men. And if there are not two men [available], then a man and two women from those whom you accept as witnesses - so that if one of the women errs, then the other can remind her. And let not the witnesses refuse when they are called upon. And do not be [too] weary to write it, whether it is small or large, for its [specified] term. That is more just in the sight of Allah and stronger as evidence and more likely to prevent doubt between you, except when it is an immediate transaction which you conduct among yourselves. For [then] there is no blame upon you if you do not write it. And take witnesses when you conclude a contract. Let no scribe be harmed or any witness. For if you do so, indeed, it is [grave] disobedience in you. And fear Allah. And Allah teaches you. And Allah is Knowing of all things.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

bibleVerses.text: This is the thing which Yahweh does command concerning the daughters of Zelophehad, saying, ‘Let them be married to whom they think best; only they shall marry into the family of the tribe of their father. So shall no inheritance of the children of Israel move from tribe to tribe; for the children of Israel shall all keep the inheritance of the tribe of his fathers. Every daughter who possesses an inheritance in any tribe of the children of Israel shall be wife to one of the family of the tribe of her father, that the children of Israel may each possess the inheritance of his fathers. So shall no inheritance move from one tribe to another tribe; for the tribes of the children of Israel shall each keep his own inheritance.’”

One witness shall not rise up against a man for any iniquity, or for any sin, in any sin that he sins. At the mouth of two witnesses, or at the mouth of three witnesses, shall a matter be established.
But if he doesn’t listen, take one or two more with you, that at the mouth of two or three witnesses every word may be established.

Jacob said, “First, sell me your birthright.” Esau said, “Behold, I am about to die. What good is the birthright to me?” Jacob said, “Swear to me first.” He swore to him. He sold his birthright to Jacob. Jacob gave Esau bread and stew of lentils. He ate and drank, rose up, and went his way. So Esau despised his birthright.

Dare any of you, having a matter against his neighbor, go to law before the unrighteous, and not before the saints? Don’t you know that the saints will judge the world? And if the world is judged by you, are you unworthy to judge the smallest matters? Don’t you know that we will judge angels? How much more, things that pertain to this life? If then, you have to judge things pertaining to this life, do you set them to judge who are of no account in the assembly?

One of the multitude said to him, “Teacher, tell my brother to divide the inheritance with me.”
But he said to him,
“Man, who made me a judge or an arbitrator over you?”
but he shall acknowledge the firstborn, the son of the hated, by giving him a double portion of all that he has; for he is the beginning of his strength. The right of the firstborn is his.
But if anyone doesn’t provide for his own, and especially his own household, he has denied the faith, and is worse than an unbeliever.

faq.question: Why does Islamic law give daughters half the share of sons in inheritance?
faq.answer: Islamic scholars explain this through several principles. Firstly, Islamic inheritance law imposes financial maintenance obligations on male heirs toward female relatives. A son inherits more because he is expected to provide for sisters, daughters, and other female dependents, whereas daughters have no such obligation. Secondly, the system accounts for the historical economic roles within families. Thirdly, the Quranic verses are explicit about these proportions and understood as divine ordinance. However, contemporary Muslim scholars debate whether this principle still applies universally when women have equal economic participation and are not financially dependent on male relatives. Some Muslim-majority countries like Morocco have reformed their inheritance law to provide greater equality, arguing that Islamic principles of justice can be served through different distributions.

faq.question: Does the Quran specify different testimony requirements for women in all legal contexts?
faq.answer: No. The Quranic verse specifying the one-man-and-two-women testimony ratio (Quran 2:282) appears specifically in the context of documenting commercial debt transactions. Scholars distinguish this context from other legal matters. Many classical Islamic jurists applied different standards to different types of cases. Additionally, most modern Muslim-majority legal systems apply gender-neutral evidence standards in many legal contexts, including criminal law and civil litigation. Some scholars argue that when the underlying principle—reliability and accuracy—can be achieved through different means, the application can vary. This reflects ongoing scholarly debate about distinguishing between timeless principles and historically situated applications.

faq.question: Does the Bible provide a single comprehensive inheritance law like Islam?
faq.answer: No. Biblical inheritance law is case-based rather than formulaic. The Torah provides principles illustrated through specific cases (like the daughters of Zelophehad inheriting their father's property) rather than universal mathematical formulas. The general principle favored male heirs, particularly the firstborn son who received a double portion, but this was not absolute—daughters could inherit if no sons existed, and women were not prohibited from owning property. The flexibility of the biblical approach allowed adaptation to different circumstances, but it provided less predictability than Islamic law's universal formulas. Different biblical periods and different communities may have had varying practices regarding inheritance.

faq.question: Did Jesus provide guidance on how to handle inheritance disputes?
faq.answer: Jesus declined to arbitrate an inheritance dispute when asked. According to Luke 12:13-14, someone asked Jesus to tell his brother to divide their inheritance with him, and Jesus responded that He was not a judge or arbitrator of such matters. Instead, Jesus redirected the person's focus to spiritual wealth and the dangers of covetousness. The New Testament generally emphasizes spiritual over material priorities and provides limited specific guidance on inheritance mechanics. However, Paul addresses obligations to provide for family members (1 Timothy 5:8), indicating that supporting relatives remains a moral duty within Christian ethics.

faq.question: What is the principle behind requiring multiple witnesses in both traditions?
faq.answer: Both Islamic and Biblical traditions recognize that a single person's word can be mistaken or dishonest, so serious claims must be corroborated. Deuteronomy 19:15 establishes that two or three witnesses must establish a matter. The Quran emphasizes documentation and multiple witnesses as means to achieve reliability and prevent disputes. The underlying principle is epistemological: truth is more reliably established when multiple independent sources corroborate it. This principle endures today in modern law through requirements for corroborating evidence, cross-examination, and the "beyond a reasonable doubt" standard. Both traditions recognize that if one person alone could determine truth, powerful people could make false accusations with impunity.

faq.question: How have modern legal systems adapted these inheritance and testimony principles?
faq.answer: Muslim-majority countries vary in their approach. Some preserve classical Islamic inheritance law; others have reformed it to provide greater gender equality while arguing that Islamic principles of justice support these changes. On testimony, most modern legal systems, including Muslim-majority ones, now apply gender-neutral evidentiary standards in most contexts. Christian-majority countries long ago moved away from biblical inheritance law toward secular systems based on testamentary freedom and formal legal procedures. On testimony, the biblical principle of requiring corroboration survives in modern evidence law through cross-examination, forensic verification, and the burden of proof. Both traditions have engaged the challenge of distinguishing between timeless principles (justice, reliability) and historically situated applications (specific formulas and procedures).

furtherReading.title: Principles of Islamic Jurisprudence
furtherReading.author: Mohammad Hashim Kamali
furtherReading.note: Provides essential context for understanding how Islamic law develops from scripture, including discussion of inheritance law principles and their theological foundations.

furtherReading.title: Studies in Qur'an and Hadith: The Formation of the Islamic Law of Inheritance
furtherReading.author: David S. Powers
furtherReading.note: Detailed scholarly examination of the formation of Quranic inheritance law and its classical interpretation by early Islamic jurists.

furtherReading.title: Women in the Qur'an, Traditions, and Interpretation
furtherReading.author: Barbara Freyer Stowasser
furtherReading.note: Scholarly analysis of Quranic passages concerning women, including inheritance and testimony, examining both classical and modern interpretations.

furtherReading.title: Law and Revolution: The Formation of the Western Legal Tradition
furtherReading.author: Harold J. Berman
furtherReading.note: Historical examination of how Biblical and canon-law principles were understood and adapted by Christian thinkers during the transition to modern secular Western legal systems.
